Troubleshooting and FAQ

Shinichi Nakagawa

Source: vignettes/troubleshooting.Rmd
troubleshooting.Rmd

Common problems and their fixes. Organised by the symptom you’re likely to see. If your issue isn’t covered here, please open an issue.

Julia and installation

julia_is_ready() hangs or returns FALSE

The first call to julia_is_ready() compiles the Julia kernel. This takes 60–90 seconds on a typical machine. Subsequent calls in the same R session are ~instant.

If it still returns FALSE after 2+ minutes:

  1. Verify Julia is installed: system("julia --version") in R.
  2. Verify JuliaConnectoR can find it: JuliaConnectoR::juliaSetupOk().
  3. On macOS, Julia installed via Homebrew can have PATH issues. Install via juliaup instead — curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh.
  4. If JULIA_HOME is set to an old installation, unset it: Sys.unsetenv("JULIA_HOME") then restart R.

“Package Clade not found” or similar Julia-side errors

The Julia environment lives in inst/julia/. If clade was recently updated (e.g. you pulled new kernel changes), rebuild the Julia environment:

cd ~/.julia/packages/  # or wherever your R library has clade installed
cd $(R -e 'cat(system.file("julia", package = "clade"))' -s)
julia --project=. -e 'using Pkg; Pkg.instantiate()'

First-compile is extremely slow (>5 minutes)

On first compile, Julia pre-compiles all kernel modules. This is cached per-Julia-environment. If you see >5 minute compiles every time, something is invalidating the cache — often a package-manager update or a stale Manifest.toml. Clean with:

using Pkg
Pkg.resolve()
Pkg.instantiate()

“My population crashed” — viability failures

Symptom: final n_agents near zero

Your simulation ran but the population went extinct. Almost always one of:

  • Mortality too high. Check death_prob, senescence_rate, winter_death_prob. If multiple mortality sources are on, they compound.
  • Food too scarce. Check grass_rate (per-tick regrowth) and grass_max. If grass_rate is below ~0.1 the world can starve.
  • Too few initial agents combined with high stochasticity. Try n_agents_init = 40L minimum for a 30×30 grid.
  • Reproduction threshold too high relative to foraging rate. Check min_repro_energy vs. what an agent can accumulate in its lifetime.

Always use viability_report() before interpreting a result. Extinct populations have no signal to report:

rd  <- get_run_data(env)
via <- viability_report(rd)
print(via)   # populations survived? heritability non-zero?

Symptom: populations survive but show n_agents oscillating wildly

Usually Allee effects + small grid. Two fixes:

  1. Use realistic_specs() or ultra_realistic_specs() — these scale the grid and initial population to reduce Allee stochasticity.
  2. Relax min_repro_energy slightly, or increase max_ticks so the population has time to recover between crashes.

Parallel runs and batch_alife()

“All connections are in use” or hanging mclapply

Never use parallel::mclapply() with clade. mclapply forks the R process; JuliaConnectoR doesn’t survive a fork and deadlocks. batch_alife() uses PSOCK by default — that’s the only safe backend.

PSOCK workers don’t pick up kernel changes

PSOCK workers use the installed clade package, not your devtools::load_all() development tree. If you changed Julia kernel files in inst/julia/ and tried a PSOCK batch, you’ll see stale behaviour.

Fix: reinstall before launching:

devtools::install(quick = TRUE)   # then restart R and the batch job

“All 64 connections are in use” on a big machine

PSOCK has per-machine limits on the number of parallel R connections. On this project’s machine we cap at n_cores = 64 even when more physical cores exist. If you need more, either:

  1. Split the job across multiple R sessions manually.
  2. Use the future package with a multisession plan (more robust than raw PSOCK, but slightly slower startup).

“Direction right, magnitude wrong”

What it means

The audit ledger uses three tiers:

  • ✅ PASS — direction correct, |t| ≥ 2 at the tested number of seeds
  • 🟠 Passed-consistent — direction correct but |t| < 2
  • 🔴 Contradicts — direction opposite to theory

If your result is 🟠, usually one of these is going on:

  1. Noise dominates: more seeds reveal a real signal. Scale from 8 → 16 → 32 seeds before concluding anything.
  2. Kernel-limited: the mechanism you need isn’t in clade at sufficient strength. Check whether the module has a “strength” parameter that can be cranked up (e.g. habitat_preference_strength, parasite_pressure).
  3. Honest null: theory is wrong at this parameter regime. Document it. The audit ledger has several honest nulls — they’re not failures, they’re findings.

“Signal vanishes at realistic scale”

This is a real phenomenon, not a bug. Some signals (e.g. Hamilton 1971 selfish-herd) depend on population size via 1/√N and literally become smaller at larger scales. Check the audit notes for the relevant scenario — they flag where scale matters.

Spec conflicts

“kin_selection does nothing” / “cooperation does nothing”

Many modules require other modules to be on. Common dependencies:

  • kin_selection needs ploidy = 2 (diploid). It’s meaningless under haploid reproduction.
  • mate_choice needs signal_mating or signal_evolution_drift to have something to choose on.
  • parental_care needs neonatal_foraging_deficit > 0 to have a demographic reason to care.
  • coevolving_parasites needs signal_dims > 0 for continuous- trait matching, or n_parasite_loci > 0 for Hamming-haplotype.
  • cooperative_breeding requires cooperation_evolution = TRUE plus a delayed-dispersal mechanism (e.g. saturated habitat).

The Parameter reference documents these dependencies per flag.

“stress_hypermutation produces no signal”

stress_threshold must be above min_repro_energy or the module is structurally silent — agents never reach a stress state before reproducing. The default is set conservatively. For stress-scarcity studies, try stress_threshold = 150 with default min_repro_energy = 120.

RNG / reproducibility

“My runs don’t reproduce even with random_seed”

clade’s kernel uses random_seed but does NOT fully reset its RNG between run_alife() calls within one R session. Consecutive runs share state. If you need bit-exact reproducibility:

  1. Restart R between runs, or
  2. Use batch_alife() with explicit seeds = ... — each worker gets a fresh Julia environment.

Figure scripts don’t reproduce the audit

The figure-generation scripts in vignettes/ and the audit scripts in dev/audit/fidelity/ must call run_alife() in the same order to reproduce the exact same population. If you refactor call order, expect seed-level mismatch even with identical random_seed.

Getting help

If your problem isn’t here:

  1. Check the Scenarios index — your question may match an audited scenario.
  2. Check the Kernel as biology articles for mechanism-level documentation.
  3. Skim open issues — someone may have hit the same wall.
  4. Open a new issue with:
    • sessionInfo() output
    • The specs list you ran (redact anything sensitive)
    • The error message / unexpected behaviour
    • What you expected vs. what you got

Anti-patterns to avoid

  • Single-seed conclusions. Never report a result from one seed. 8 minimum for pilots, 16+ for claims.
  • Deleting the viability_report() check because “the result looks reasonable”. A dead population produces plausible-looking but meaningless final-state numbers.
  • Citing clade without citing the underlying theory. clade is the instrument; the theory (Hamilton, Williams, DeWitt, etc.) is the question. Always cite both.
  • Running pkgdown::build_site() on CI before running devtools::check() locally — the build site rebuilds vignettes and will fail in non-obvious ways if your specs have typos.
  • git add . in a clade working directory. There are often large cached .rds files that you don’t want to commit. Always add specific files.

On this page
